items.question_text: Under 8-4-1, within the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace, use _____ NM between aircraft whose flight paths are defined by published Grid System waypoints.
items.explanation: Per 8-4-1, 12 NM is used in the Gulf Grid System.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-1, in which sections of Chapter 8 can regional ICAO lateral separation minima be found?
items.explanation: Per the note, lateral separation minima are in Sections 7 (North Atlantic), 8 (Caribbean), 9 (Pacific), and 10 (North American-Arctic CTA).

choices.text: Sections 5, 6, 7, and 8.
Sections 3, 4, 5, and 6.
Sections 7, 8, 9, and 10.
Sections 1, 2, 3, and 4.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-1, how is the Grid System defined?
items.explanation: Per the note, the Grid System is those waypoints contained within the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace and published on the IFR Vertical Flight Reference Chart.

choices.text: NAVAID-defined intersections on all low-altitude en route charts.
Any waypoints that are spaced at least 10 NM apart in any offshore airspace.
Waypoints defined by radar coverage areas along the entire Gulf Coast.
Waypoints in the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace published on the IFR Vertical Flight Reference Chart.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-2, for the oceanic boundary transition method, flight paths must diverge by at least _____ degrees until the larger minimum is established.
items.explanation: Per 8-4-2a3(b), flight paths must diverge by 15 degrees or more.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-2, for intersecting flight paths with constant and same width protected airspace, what distance establishes lateral separation?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-2b, separation exists when either aircraft is at or beyond a distance equal to the applicable lateral separation minimum measured perpendicular to the flight path of the other aircraft.

choices.text: The average of the two aircrafts' protected airspace widths.
The sum of the protected airspace of both flight paths, same as the different-width rule.
Twice the applicable lateral separation minimum.
The applicable lateral separation minimum measured perpendicular to the flight path of the other aircraft.

items.question_text: A trainee argues that for intersecting flight paths with constant but different width protected airspace, the applicable minimum is the larger individual width. Is this correct under 8-4-2?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-2c, the sum of the protected airspace of both flight paths is used, not the larger individual width alone.

choices.text: Yes — the larger individual width alone is correct.
No — the sum of the protected airspace of both flight paths is used, not just the larger individual width.
Yes — but only when both aircraft are at the same altitude.
No — the smaller individual width is used for efficiency.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-3, below FL 240, reduce the width of protected airspace to _____ miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 57.14 miles from the NAVAID.
items.explanation: Per 8-4-3a, below FL240 the reduced width is 5 miles on each side.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-3, after the reduced-width segment, protected airspace increases on a _____ degree angle from the route centerline, measured at the NAVAID.
items.explanation: Per 8-4-3a and 8-4-3b, both altitude bands use a 5-degree widening angle.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-3, at and above FL240, what is the distance from the NAVAID to which the reduced width extends before widening?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-3b, the reduced width extends to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID before widening.

choices.text: 57.14 miles.
100 miles.
114.29 miles.
150 miles.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-4, for dead reckoning track separation, the tracks must diverge by at least _____ degrees and one aircraft must be at least 15 miles from the point of intersection.
items.explanation: Per 8-4-4c1, DR tracks must diverge by at least 45 degrees.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-4, how does the NDB track separation divergence requirement compare to the VOR requirement for same-NAVAID separation?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-4a1, VOR/VORTAC/TACAN requires at least 15 degrees divergence. Per 8-4-4a2, NDB requires at least 30 degrees divergence.

choices.text: Both require the same 15-degree minimum divergence.
NDB requires 30 degrees divergence, while VOR/VORTAC/TACAN requires 15 degrees — NDB needs twice the divergence.
NDB requires 15 degrees while VOR requires 30 degrees.
Both require 45-degree divergence for all track separation.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-4, what is the inbound limitation for same-NAVAID NDB track separation?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-4a2(a), the separation must not be used when one or both aircraft are inbound to the aid unless the distance from the facility can be readily determined by reference to the NAVAID.

choices.text: Must not be used for outbound aircraft, only inbound.
May be used for all inbound aircraft without restriction.
Must not be used when one or both aircraft are inbound to the aid unless the distance from the facility can be readily determined by reference to the NAVAID.
May only be used when both aircraft are at least 30 miles from the NAVAID.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-4, for divergence that falls between two NDB table values, what value should be used to obtain the distance?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-4a2(a), use the lesser divergence value to obtain the distance — this is conservative, requiring more distance than the next higher band.

choices.text: Use the average of the two values.
Use the greater divergence value to obtain the distance.
Interpolate between the two values.
Use the lesser divergence value to obtain the distance.

items.question_text: Where should a controller look for ICAO regional lateral separation minima specific to the North Atlantic?

choices.text: Section 3, Longitudinal Separation.
Section 4, Lateral Separation.
Section 7, North Atlantic ICAO Region.
Section 1, General.

items.explanation: Per the note in 8-4-1, regional lateral separation minima are in Sections 7 (North Atlantic), 8 (Caribbean), 9 (Pacific), and 10 (North American-Arctic CTA).

items.question_text: For variable-width protected airspace on intersecting flight paths, where is the protected airspace measured for each aircraft?

choices.text: At the departure fix measured along the outbound radial.
At the mid-point of the flight segment regardless of overlap.
Only at the NAVAID defining the route.
Perpendicular to the flight path at the first or last point of protected airspace overlap.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-2d, measure protected airspace perpendicular to the flight path at the first or last point, as applicable, of protected airspace overlap.

items.question_text: What two preconditions must be met before reducing route protected airspace under 8-4-3?

choices.text: The pilot must request reduced width and the supervisor must approve each flight.
Routes must be above FL290 and the aircraft must be RVSM-approved.
Routes must have been satisfactorily flight checked and notice must have been given to users.
The routes must be defined by GPS waypoints and within radar coverage.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-3, the routes must be satisfactorily flight checked and notice must be given to users before protected airspace reduction.

items.question_text: For track separation using different NAVAIDs under 8-4-4b, how is separation established?

choices.text: Require both aircraft to be 15 miles from their respective NAVAIDs.
Apply a 15-degree divergence requirement regardless of NAVAID type.
Use 10-minute longitudinal separation in addition to track assignments.
Assign tracks so that the protected airspace of the flight paths does not overlap.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-4b, separate aircraft using different NAVAIDs by assigning tracks so that their protected airspace does not overlap.
